issue_comment_body: You're a genius. Calling `Gtk.init(sys.argv)` right after import fixes the problem!
OK I see the problem. It's really an OS problem, but one that I should be able to work around. The issue comes down to the command:

What's I'm suggesting is that with the idiomatic way to enable `pook` you need to move [these lines](https://github.com/mindflayer/python-mocket/blob/master/mocket/plugins/pook_mock_engine.py#L45-L58) in your implementation when the mocks are actually triggered and exists.

Currently, no mocks will be registered because you're registering them before declaring them because of the mock engine activation order.

The idiomatic way of using `pook` would be:
```js
# Registering the mock engine at global level, once, would be the natural approach
pook.set_mock_engine(MocketEngine)

@pook.on  # -> pook will activate the underlying mock engine when the function is called but we still don't have any mock already declared
def my_test_fn():
   # Then we declare our mocks
   pook.get('foo.com', reply=404)
   pook.get('bar.com', reply=404)

   # Then we run our requests
   requests.get('http://foo.com')
   requests.get('http://bar.com')
   
   # After function execution, we're done!
```

The issue lies with [how backports.functools_lru_cache is resolved as a dependency](https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/jaraco.functools/src/666b189f38d913544746ba40f97d1e9f116496c9/setup.py?at=default&fileviewer=file-view-default#setup.py-32:34). The package is required conditionally on the Python version being 2.7. I suspect that the version of pip you have isn't honoring that dependency properly.

I just installed jaraco.functools with pip 7.1.2 and it installed backports.functools_lru_cache as required.

I suspect you either need to upgrade pip or install backports.functools_lru_cache manually (i.e. `pip install backports.functools_lru_cache`).

Let me know if that doesn't solve it.

A workaround

```
class _BugFixMixin(object):
    
    def send(self, message):
        txZMQ.ZmqConnection.send(self, message)
        # XXX: sometimes, send operation is completed and data is available
        # for reading before we goes into select, namely, reactor
        # in this case, select can't catch a read event from fd
        # to solve the problem, we should check is there data to read here
        # it will clean the POLLIN event of the socket, it makes
        # the fd will be notified if there is event later
        reactor.callFromThread(txZMQ.ZmqConnection.doRead, self)
```

example usage:

```
class ZmqXREQConnection(_BugFixMixin, txZMQ.ZmqXREQConnection):
   pass
```

To make the echo server example a little more realistic, I added a 1 ms busy loop to the send/receive loop, to simulate it doing a bit of on-CPU processing to each message ([code](https://gist.github.com/njsmith/c7d64eb04e83be34c0b56ecb75e5aca9)). With this change, the output becomes:
```
~/src/curio$ python instrumented-echo-server-with-delay-loop.py
Connection from ('127.0.0.1', 58964)
Connection from ('127.0.0.1', 59144)
Switched after 8851 loops (12426.1 ms)
Switched after 1888 loops (2599.1 ms)
Switched after 5412 loops (7557.6 ms)
Switched after 25424 loops (35197.3 ms)
Switched after 175 loops (255.5 ms)
Switched after 35181 loops (49042.2 ms)
Switched after 15333 loops (20871.3 ms)
Switched after 25979 loops (35952.4 ms)
Switched after 8936 loops (12574.0 ms)
...
```

> How are other frameworks dealing with this? How do programs that use threads deal with this? 

With threads this can't happen because the scheduler interrupt will fire, the scheduler will notice that the eager thread has outlived its allocated time-slice, and it'll get pre-empted and bumped to the back of the line. Then the next time the eager thread is running, if another some I/O suddenly arrives for another thread that's blocked on I/O, the eager thread will get pre-empted to let the I/O bound thread run, because the I/O bound thread hasn't been using up its timeslices so it gets preferred access to the CPU. I guess you already know all this, but maybe it's useful for those reading the thread later :-).

> I'm not yet convinced that this would actually be a common situation

I don't actually find this very reassuring, because even if it's rare/probabilistic... if you give me a choice between a server that usually handles requests in 1 ms but sometimes has a 100 ms latency spike, versus one that always handles requests in 5 ms, then I think almost everyone will prefer the 5 ms server. To me it doesn't make sense to talk about average case latencies until after at least putting a bound on tail latencies.

All that's required to hit this is a program that is occasionally CPU-bound and occasionally has fast peers. That seems pretty realistic to me :-/

> I don't want to completely discount the performance overhead of constantly doing everything on the event loop by doing away with eager read/write.

I don't either, but I guess my inclination would be to focus on getting the semantics right first. Premature optimization is the root of all evil... or specifically, spending energy on optimizing now seems like a poor investment compared to working on correctness, because correctness is what lets us start writing apps on top of curio, and apps on top of curio are what let us start nailing down the semantics, and are also what give us real benchmarks to replace this echo server stuff. And it's a much easier to try and optimize a fixed set of semantics with real benchmarks than it is to try to optimize an unstable set of semantics without real benchmarks.

From a quick check it looks like fixing this in the most crude way possible (adding a `curio.sleep(0)` next to every eager read/write) only costs about ~15% in the echo server microbenchmark? We'll make that up easily later if/when we get serious about optimizing things (like, writing a libuv backend, or switching to pypy3, or...).

You are right, we always want some ``F(x1, x2, ..., xm; p1, p2, ..., po) -> R^n`` which has to be defined. The main difference for a system of ODE's is that F has to be build from ``d(x_i)/dt = f(t, x_1, ..., x_n; p_1, ..., p_n)`` in the case of a symbolic ODE, which means a generic way has to be found to build ``F``. Once this has been done every F is the same, which is what I meant with a model is a model is a model.

When you specify your own ``F`` from python code (programmed function) you can do whatever floats your boat. What makes ODE's special is that we would like to have the syntactic sugar since most of them can be written symbolically.

Of course it would be nice if a nice integrator API can be defined so you can reuse that for programmed functions.

"well defined" for me has a very loose definition which is best interpreted as 'whatever works in the current context'. I'm a physicist after all ;).

And github markdown doesn't support any kind of math mode, which was a disappointment for me too. Apparently it's due to safety considerations since you won't be surprised to hear that running latex introduces some security issues.

```python

```python

view._ngl_displayed_callbacks[1](view)
view._ngl_displayed_callbacks[2](view)

```python

view._ngl_displayed_callbacks[5](view)
```
Removing callback 4 even if I keep the rest gives me the correct coloring! So callback 4 is the evil guy.

```python

view._ngl_displayed_callbacks[4](view)
view._ngl_displayed_callbacks[5](view)
# Cell 2 :  Works!!!

```

So what is going on here? I would assume it has to do with sending commands to the JS viewer. Could it be @arose that the commands are put in some buffer and then executed in a possibly random order?

Well, I think I found the source of the problem. Turns out I wasn't always sorting the events alphabetically as intended when generating the simultaneous pairs. `extract_events` returned an unsorted set that was directly iterated over by `occurrence` to generate simultaneous pairs. Anyway, I'm sure you aren't that interested in my code.

Point is, the problem was not actually in pyeda or boolexpr after all. Thanks for your help.

The standard for JWT defines an `exp` claim for expiration. The expiration is represented as a NumericDate:

> A JSON numeric value representing the number of seconds from 1970-01-01T00:00:00Z UTC until the specified UTC date/time, ignoring leap seconds. This is equivalent to the IEEE Std 1003.1, 2013 Edition [POSIX.1] definition "Seconds Since the Epoch", in which each day is accounted for by exactly 86400 seconds, other than that non-integer values can be represented. See RFC 3339 [RFC3339] for details regarding date/times in general and UTC in particular.

This means that the `exp` field should contain the number of seconds since the epoch. We currently convert `exp` datetime to an int here:

https://github.com/jpadilla/pyjwt/blob/7f7d524b78ab98e42e9064f4f2bab5b6e126f766/jwt/api_jwt.py#L43-L46

Hi!

Great question (and sorry for the delay in replying).

This project is mostly a test case for parser generator I'm working on, and so raw speed is less important to me than being able to actually generate a correct parser cleanly from a grammar.
That said, a generated grammar this slow is *almost* useless, so I do need to make sure the generator generates much better code.

Unsurprisingly, the builtin json module has both been tuned to be as fast as possible in pure Python, and has a C extension to be even faster. While I might eventually be able to generate a C extension using the parser generator, that's a ways off on the roadmap.

The pure python builtin JSON parser is 6-10x slower than the C parser; currently, I think my generated code is about 160x - 200x slower than the builtin pure python version.

I think I can get the generated code to be a lot better, and the current dev version I have on the parser can produce a JSON parser that is only 10x slower than the pure python version; this appears to be comparable to what you'd get with PLY and significantly faster than an ANTLR-generated JSON parser. I expect I should be able to match this for JSON5 without too much more work.

That's a long way of saying I expect I can get this 10-20x faster fairly soon now, maybe another week or two depending on how much free time I can find. 

I don't know how hard it will be to get that last 10x, to match the pure python implementation, or even if it's doable at all, and I have no ETA or even a real expectation that I'll be able to match the speed of the native JSON implementation, but I do plan to keep working on it and I will post updates with my progress.

Hopefully that answers your question, probably in much more detail than you were hoping for. I hope the answer is not too disappointing.

I think your program is registering the hotkeys correctly but then exiting because it finished executing all statements.

Try to add a `input()` at the end or something similar to ensure it stops. Or test those lines in the interpreter, where it won't exit by itself.

Some other problems you may run into:

- Using `quit` as hotkey callback may not work as expected because the library uses multiple threads to read events, and the quit may just exit one of them.
- The second hotkey doesn't look right. The hotkey description is ok, but your callback is a string. Hotkeys expect an executable callback, some function that will be called. You may have confused it with an abbreviation that replaces one string for another. For that you can copy the example hotkey, the one with the `write`.

In pyopenvr, the method name is `triggerHapticPulse`, not `TriggerHapticPulse` (note capitalization). In general, pyopenvr uses method names to begin with a lower case letter, unlike the C++ OpenVR API, because that seems more Pythonic to me.

One way to see what methods are available in the IVRSystem class, for example, is to type
`>>> help(openvr.IVRSystem)`
at the python command prompt.

Also, just like in the regular C++ OpenVR API, you should probably be calling `openvr.VRSystem().triggerHapticPulse(...)` (i.e. without the "I") rather than `openvr.IVRSystem().triggerHapticPulse(...)`, to use the shared global singleton VRSystem() instance, rather than creating a newly constructed IVRSystem() every time.

I have actually tested the triggerHapticPulse method, but I expect it will work in pyopenvr exactly as it does when using the C++ OpenVR API.

In summary, use a lower case "t" and remove the "I", and you should get further.

> An alternative would be an intermediate parser/converter script that smashes multiple csv files into a sane format (eg, the 4-tuple of start, end, label, level described above), and then we can go back to the simpler eval script format. I prefer this approach, but it's still hackish IMO.

I think that's a perfectly valid approach. Just provide a helper script that's specifically targeted at preprocessing the SALAMI annotations, and tell users to write their predictions in the sane format right from the start. Then `./task_eval.py ref_file est_file arguments` would be enough.

To clarify what I've written before, I think that Python users may not need a runnable `task_eval.py` script (that dictates the I/O format to use), because they can use the evaluation functions directly. So whatever file format you choose for `task_eval.py` should be easy to handle in other languages (such as Matlab or Java).

It doesn't set `in_tag = False`, because it has advanced the parser to the start of the next field (and tag) in line 153 where the `point + raw_len + 1` advances over the value and the SOH.

Have a look at the two relevant unit tests:`test_raw_data()` and `test_raw_data_tags()`.  In both these cases, the message has a Checksum (10) tag following the raw data field, and they appear to parse correctly (and, to that point, both those tests fail with your patch).

> I'm currently getting the annotations from the parsed code. Alternatively one could ask the python interpreter and use the .__annotations__. Which is preferred?

Jedi only supports the parser, you cannot just magically use `__annotations__`.
However we still want type inference there. What I would suggest is something
that already exists within Jedi in some other forms (e.g. `py__mro__`,
`py__bases`, etc) - we recreate `__annotations__` in a static analysis kind of
way (https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi/blob/linter/jedi/evaluate/representation.py#L17):

'
```

class Function():  # In evaluate.representation
    @memoize_default()
    def py__annotations__(self):
        parser_func = self.base
        dct = {'return': self._evaluator.eval_element(parser_func.annotation)}
        for param in parser_func.params:
            dct[param.name.value] = self._evaluator.eval_element(param.annotation)
        return dct
```

With this function you basically have everything you need.

>>  It seems useful to create .pycs when doing install?
> pip will do that on the actual install.

Please bear with me here; there were a lot of issues with these tables in the past. The problem is, if there's no .pyc in the install directory, the .pyc is created locally where *users* of pycparser live, which is very bad because pycparser is used transitively but a lot of tools today.

Currently `_build_tables` is run during installation to generate new .py files that weren't shipped. I'm not 100% sure pip actually does that, since this is a custom step that happens after the actual installation.

Therefore, at the very least I wouldn't run with `-B` during `install`, perhaps only during `sdist`.

from django.utils import six
from rest_framework_tracking.mixins import LoggingMixin

def tracking_api(logging_methods=[], sensitive_fields={}):
    """
    This decorator must by install before api_view decorator. Eg:

    @tracking_api(['GET', 'POST'])
    @api_view(['GET'])
    def view_packs(request):
        ...
    """
    http_logging_methods = ['GET'] if (logging_methods is None) else logging_methods

    def decorator(func):

        TrackingWrappedAPIView = type(
            six.PY3 and 'TrackingWrappedAPIView' or b'TrackingWrappedAPIView',
            (LoggingMixin, func.view_class),
            {'__doc__': func.__doc__})

        TrackingWrappedAPIView.logging_methods = http_logging_methods

        if sensitive_fields:
            TrackingWrappedAPIView.sensitive_fields = sensitive_fields

        return TrackingWrappedAPIView.as_view()
    return decorator
```

Is something like this what you are thinking of:

```python
class Parent(Model):
    __tablename__ = 'parent'
    id = Column(types.Integer(), primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    children = orm.relation('Association')

class Child(Model):
    __tablename__ = 'child'
    id = Column(types.Integer(), primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)

class Association(Model):
    __tablename__ = 'association'
    parent_id = Column(types.Integer(), ForeignKey('parent.id'), primary_key=True)
    child_id = Column(types.Integer(), ForeignKey('child.id'), primary_key=True)
    child = orm.relation('Child')

parent = Parent(children=[{'child': {'id': _id}} for _id in range(1, 5)])
db.save(parent)
```

which roughly results in the following SQL (using sqlite):

```sql
INSERT INTO parent DEFAULT VALUES
()
INSERT INTO child (id) VALUES (?)
((1,), (2,), (3,), (4,))
INSERT INTO association (parent_id, child_id) VALUES (?, ?)
((1, 1), (1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4))
COMMIT
```

That would cover the case where you're just creating new children. It will fail if you need to associate existing children or remove/add children from a parent _unless_ you have modified the relationships to be something like:

```python
class Parent(Model):
    ...
    children = orm.relation('Association', cascade='all, delete-orphan')

class Association(Model):
    ...
    child = orm.relation('Child', cascade='all')
```

However, this will result in `Child` records being deleted if they are removed from a parent (which may be undesirable).

A safer approach if `Child` records can live on their own independent of their parent(s) would be to fetch the `Child` model instances from the database and use those in place of `children=[{'child': child1}, {'child': child2}]` (i.e. where `child1 = db.query(Child).get(1) or Child(id=1)` or something similar). There may be some other edge cases depending on your relationship schema/logic you are modeling.

Given that there are many different ways that M:M relationships can be structured, sqlservice only tries to create new instances of models for relationships when mapping `dict` values given to `Model.update()`. So it's entirely possible to pass nested relationship attributes to `update()` but it's up to the user to implement logic for properly handling relationship mappings if they are more complicated than anything beyond "always create a new model instance for relationships when raw dict values used".

BTW, the implementation for this can be found in [Model.__set_relationship_field](https://github.com/dgilland/sqlservice/blob/6a789bf7aa8b1295ca318b05ee531805dfa21b81/sqlservice/model.py#L96) and more specifically [here](https://github.com/dgilland/sqlservice/blob/6a789bf7aa8b1295ca318b05ee531805dfa21b81/sqlservice/model.py#L112). That part is where a new relationship model instance is created which won't be associated with any record in the database (an alternative here might be to attempt to look up an existing record using the primary key value of the new relationship instance but that is beyond what sqlservice would try to do).

Does all this address your original question or have I gone in the wrong direction? Is there a different approach/enhancement to sqlservice that might better meet your use-case?

Thank you for your contribution.

I went with a slightly different implementation I did here: https://github.com/kata198/func_timeout/commit/00685ec5465670085538734e9fcf369093d51e1a 

And included a unit test (fails before change, passes after) which tests every distinct combination of the _func\_timeout_ decorator.

Since decorators are just functions, my implementation calls "wraps(func)" which returns a decorator which will insert the correct meta info, and we explicitly call that returned function on our decorator, like so:

    return wraps(func)(_function_wrapper)

I chose this instead of rewriting the code to be able to use the decorator syntax as there is a particular issue involving StoppableThread wherein you could get the wrong traceback (sometimes doubled-up) in a certain set of circumstances. The way the decorators are written now resolves that issue. It's kind of hairy because python doesn't officially support stopping threads at all, the way func_timeout implements it is unique (we raise an exception in the python context such that the next python lib call in C will terminate execution with a special BaseException type). Since this is not a supported operation (though this implementation DOES WORK), it is subject to some weirdness.

So in summary, your commit is not wrong, and I credited you in both the ChangeLog and commit message, but I used a slightly different implementation to retain the careful balance that is StoppableThread and exceptions.

To be honest I didnt't completely read PEP343.
But I found this:

> he motivation for this
    detail is to make it possible for `mgr.__exit__()` to swallow
    exceptions, without making it too easy (since the default return
    value, None, is false and this causes the exception to be
    re-raised).

In my opinion it's not a good Idea to always return `True` from `__exit__()`
In most many cases I want to do something with exceptions during transactions (retry, show error to user .... )

Also in PEP343:

>  If the
    finally-suite was reached through normal completion of BLOCK or
    through a non-local goto (a break, continue or return statement in
    BLOCK), mgr.__exit__() is called with three None arguments.  If
    the finally-suite was reached through an exception raised in
    BLOCK, mgr.__exit__() is called with three arguments representing
    the exception type, value, and traceback.

So I think you should not return `True` unless all 3 call parameters are `None` or you handle the exception explicitly.

All of this is just my thinking, I'm not an expert here.

Looks like, there are two problems: encoding and JSON-format.

# Encoding problem
The encoding error can be avoidable by passing appropriate encoding to `--encoding` option:

```
$ sqlitebiter file --encoding "ISO 8859-7" data.json
```

# JSON format problem
JSON data format in the `data.zip` does not support for sqlitebiter.
Details are described in http://sqlitebiter.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/usage/file/index.html

This is just a drive-by opinion, as I'm not a maintainer and am just subscribed to this repo out of interest. But I wanted to share my experience with maintaining debian directories. I've taken this advice before and maintained debian directories on separate branches or separate repos entirely. In all cases I've found it to be a lot of trouble for no gain. The debian directory actually gets out of date _faster_, because it's out of sight from the rest of the code.

The only time I think it makes sense to split out the debian directory is when you cannot collaborate with the upstream maintainer to keep the debian directory up to date.

I think that FAQ is referring to shipping a debian directory in your distribution tarball, which is indeed a no-no. However, the FAQ specifically says that it's OK to check-in the debian directory to your version control repo. Just make sure it isn't packaged up when you run `python setup.py sdist`.

Hmm, the pattern I've tended to use elsewhere has been to provide a final arg on functions that allows you to override any settings. This enables you to use globals as sane defaults, and also easily override them for high-level integration tests - you can also mock or rewrite stuff, but I generally prefer to avoid mutating things.

Something like...
```python
render_component(
    # ..., 
    setting_overrides={
        'RENDER_URL': '...'
    },
)
```

It'd probably be worth changing the signatures to something like

```python
def render_component(path, props=None, to_static_markup=False, renderer=render_server, setting_overrides=None):
    # ...

class RenderServer(object):
    # ...
    def render(self, path, props=None, to_static_markup=False, setting_overrides=None):
        # ...
```

You end up with a bit of a dance to check the overrides and fallback to the globals, but that's easily abstracted into a function.

I had the same issue and as far as I can see the squashed migration is the reason for the problems. Any installation that earlier used south for migrations will need to use --fake-initial to install the new migration files, if not Django migrations will complain that the table already exist.
However as the squashed file replaced the initial one the entire squashed file will be faked, this means that the database schema will no longer match models.py definition.

I think it was a mistake to squash all the migrations, the `0001` migration should have been left out as it provided the first compatibility point for those with existing databases.

However to get around this the trick is to skip the squashed migration for now. The steps I used was:

```
# 1. Optional step, reset migrations to before the first migration
$ python manage.py migrate --fake reversion zero

# 2. Remove 0001_squashed_0004_auto_20160611_1202.py* from reversion/migrations, locate it in the site-packages folder in your virtual-env or global installation.

# 3. Run the first migration with --fake-initial, this marks the migration as being applied if the tables already exist.
$ python manage.py migrate --fake-initial reversion 0001

# 4. Run the rest of the migrations
$ python manage.py migrate reversion

# 5. Reinstall django-reversion to get the squashed file back, for install previous version and then back up again, for instance I used:
$ pip install django-reversion==2.0.12
$ pip install django-reversion==2.0.13
```

`showmigrations` should now show that the squashed file is applied (as it replaces all the existing migrations)

```
$ python manage.py showmigrations
reversion
 [X] 0001_squashed_0004_auto_20160611_1202 (4 squashed migrations)
```

On some operations (e.g. union or intersection), python-croaring is approximately as efficient as pyroaring.

On some other operations (e.g. constructors, iterators or subset test), python-croaring is very slow. Maybe the reason is simply because the iterations are done on Python side and not on CRoaring side (e.g. [the constructor](https://github.com/sunzhaoping/python-croaring/blob/8208469f461c3673d472835f6894a0df61503c1f/croaring/__init__.py#L111-L115) iterates on all the elements, and the method `__le__` is not implemented so the subset test will also automatically iterate on all the elements).

Thank you very very very much, this worked perfectly! Here's the working code for posterity:

```
import os
import pandas as pd
from PIL import Image
import imagehash
import numpy

DIR = '/Users/greg/Desktop'

hashes = []
for filename in ['a.jpg', 'b.jpg', 'c.jpg']:
  with open(os.path.join(DIR, filename)) as f:
    hashes.append(imagehash.whash(Image.open(f)).hash.flatten())

hashes = numpy.array(hashes)

with open(os.path.join(DIR, 'needle.jpg')) as f:
  needle = imagehash.whash(Image.open(f)).hash.flatten()

print numpy.count_nonzero(needle != hashes, axis=1)
```

Changing from the Pandas Series to the NumPy array, as you suggested, was actually necessary in order for the code above to work.

I also found out that the `axis` argument to `numpy.count_nonzero` was added relatively recently. In the older version I was running locally, passing `axis` did not do anything, nor did numpy warn me that the argument was being silently dropped...

In any and all case, this is great! Thanks again.

A good first step would be a `homematic.put_paramset` service. It would be cool if multiple values could be set at once in that case, maybe a little like this:

```yaml

If you are thinking about polling the information together with the sys vars then I think that this is probably not going to work. AFAIK you get the CCU variables through JSONRPC and I just did a little testing and could not get the WEEK_PROGRAM_POINTER through the JSONRPC API.

Another approach would be to call getParamset() whenever the thermostat device changes. Especially if putParamset() triggers a device update (haven't tested that yet) this would be an instant update - no polling required. And the API would stay very clean for users. But yes, this would lead to one extra API call per thermostat device.

But in the end I'd say that _reading_ that value is not overly important. Especially if it's so hard to make this fit with the current design of pyhomematic. It might also be easier to leave the polling entirely up to the user, but I don't know if something like `homematic.get_paramset` would actually make sense though.

instead of ```pbounds = {'a': (0, 5), 'b': (0, 10}``` with the condition `b < a`, you could change coordinates to: `x = a` and `y = b - a`, and define `pbounds = {'x': (0, 5), 'y': (0, 5)}`, and call function like so: `f(a=x, b=x + y)`.

Ah yes! Thank you for bringing that to my attention, @ApproximateIdentity. Here's how they work:

1. `coef_penalizer` is shrinkage on the absolute value of the hazard coefficients. Ex: it will shrink `c_{i,t}` towards 0.
2. `smoothing_penalizer` is shrinkage on the absolute value *between* adjacent coefficients (over time). Ex: it will shrink `c_{i,t} - c_{i, t+1}` towards 0.

Hello!
This is the intended behavior, it was changed in [hvad 1.4.0](https://django-hvad.readthedocs.io/en/latest/public/release_notes.html#id3). The exact reasons are deep changes in Django ORM in version 1.9, preventing `FallbackQueryset.use_fallbacks()` from working at all on this version (and newer).
This forced hvad to push a change that had long been delayed, that is, dropping the inefficient `FallbackQueryset` in favor of adding a much faster JOIN clause to queries. This has the side-effect of hiding models that have no translation at all.

Such models have always been considered errors anyway (older hvad versions show them, but log an error everytime one is encountered). And hvad2 will prevent them altogether.

I am actually wondering how you create them (admin should not let you create them for instance), and what you use them for?

Jonathan

Frst of all, in your example you are talking about a 2 second ping interval. The default interval for Socket.IO is 25 seconds. The client sends a ping every 25s, then waits 60s for the response. It sounds like you are using values that are much much shorter than the defaults. Is there a reason for that?

Second, while I understand the backlog of messages situation that you are describing. And your change gives the server more time to respond without closing the connection due to a ping timeout. What I don't follow is how can you make the client wait more for a delayed pong response. If the client sends a ping at some time t, but the server only receives it at t+d (with d being some long delay), the only way you can prevent the client from closing the connection is by making the ping timeout larger than the maximum possible d. This is sort of accounted for in the 25s interval vs. the 60s timeout. If things are backed up in a way that makes the server unable to respond to a ping in less than 60s, then I think there are bigger problems in the application that need to be addressed.

The main python thread is protected by the try-catch created by the API_BEGIN() and API_END() macros in src/c_api_common.h.  Any exceptions thrown in separate worker threads are not caught by this try-catch, so those exceptions cause terminate() to be called which kills the CI.  One thing I considered was setting up MXNET_ENGINE_TYPE=NaiveEngine which performs all actions on the one python thread.  The problem with this approach is that the Engine is a singleton and the environment variable is only checked when the first test of the CI calls for an engine. Once again, one needs a separate process to get a fresh engine singleton for a particular test.

So I've today finished a bit of polishing on the way to create the separate test process.  I've confirmed that the test runs properly under both Win and Linux Python3 CI environments, and is appropriately skipped for Python2 environments, where the needed fork-exec support is missing.

I'm satisfied with the PR as it stands now, and hope it will be considered for merging.

Hi - thank you for all the PRs! :-)

I don't suppose you could remove the `. r?foo` from the commit messages/PR titles? For this project we don't use that annotation (or the trailing period) since it often gets out of sync with who reviewed the PR and doesn't add much value.

We need the middleware, otherwise the `X-Frame-Options` header wouldn't be set at all (which is different to being set to `SAMEORIGIN`), which would reduce our score on https://observatory.mozilla.org/analyze.html?host=treeherder.mozilla.org (there were mentions a while back that all Mozilla sites had to get at least a 'B') - and presumably allow certain attack scenarios (though have likely they are I don't know).

async def _handle_pubsub_msg(self, pubsub_msg):
    data = json.loads(pubsub_msg.data)
    schema = self._get_and_validate_pubsub_msg_schema(data)
    # handle if it's not valid against any schema

    event_msg_data = self._parser.parse(data, schema)
    # create an event message obj (this is in the next PR, it implements `IEventMessage`)
    event_msg = self._create_gevent_msg(pubsub_msg, event_msg_data, schema)

    phase = 'enrich' if event_msg_data['resourceRecords'] else 'consume'
    self.update_phase(event_msg, phase=phase)

(which can affect partial_ratio too independently)

Not sure where the difference comes from, prob something along the lines of what you said, but note that in this example the sum of the length of the blocks (# of matches) just happens to be equal, so if you did the ratio calculation difflib's way (2.0 * # of matches / combined length) you'd still get the same ratio both times, but you get a different score the python-Levenshtein way.

>>> from fuzzywuzzy import fuzz
>>> import difflib
>>> 
>>> s1 = "the cincinnati reds vs the new york mets"
>>> s2 = "new york mets vs atlanta braves"
>>> 
>>> fuzz.SequenceMatcher(None, s1, s2).get_matching_blocks()
[(6, 0, 1), (14, 8, 1), (16, 10, 1), (18, 12, 5), (27, 21, 1), (30, 24, 1), (33, 26, 1), (37, 29, 1), (39, 30, 1), (40, 31, 0)]
>>>
>>> fuzz.SequenceMatcher = difflib.SequenceMatcher
>>>

[Match(a=27, b=0, size=13), Match(a=40, b=31, size=0)]

Thanks for the suggestion. At first blush, it sounds like an excellent idea.

In the past, I have made pre-releases available ([example](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/releases/tag/v28.2.0b1)), especially for backward-incompatible releases that were expected to cause disruption (and commensurate with the amount of disruption expected). In practice, I find that even if I cut the release and tweet about it and lead up to it with discussion and invitations to test, if issues are going to emerge, they will emerge after the release, and usually only in a small percentage of total uses.

So I've chosen instead to create minimally-invasive, rapid releases such that if issues are reported, they can be readily identified and corrected or rolled back, depending on the severity of the failure and the difficulty of corrective action. Ideally, the unit-test suite will capture all use cases, such that a release is only cut when tests pass.

This approach failed us last night when the release went out late and I managed to fall asleep before noticing any issue.

I loathe to create more manual steps that are unlikely to add significant value but guaranteed to add delays to releases.

I'd prefer to focus on how a broken build was able to bypass the existing safeguards and bolster those safeguards (test suite).

If in light of this, you still think that release-candidates are worth the trouble, we probably need more volunteers on the projects to do that additional work.

According to the [protocol documentation](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/A+Guide+To+The+Kafka+Protocol#AGuideToTheKafkaProtocol-GroupMembershipAPI), `InconsistentGroupProtocolCode` is

> returned in join group when the member provides a protocol type or set of protocols which is not compatible with the current group.

Kafka 0.10's `SyncGroupRequest` is intentionally generic to allow future extensibility. To this end, the [`MemberAssignment`](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/A+Guide+To+The+Kafka+Protocol#AGuideToTheKafkaProtocol-SyncGroupRequest) field in `SyncGroupRequest` is specified as a raw bytestring rather than providing explicit definitions for each of the fields necessary for a partition assignment. The protocol documentation stipulates that all client implementations using the "consumer" protocol type should support the schema it specifies for `MemberAssignment`. PyKafka uses the ["consumer" protocol type](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/5345019ef3a66c1c93d0c05f4d3504045324e5c6/pykafka/protocol.py#L1256) and adheres to the standard `MemberAssignment` schema.

From my read of the golang client code you're using, it looks like you're probably [also using](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/5345019ef3a66c1c93d0c05f4d3504045324e5c6/pykafka/protocol.py#L1256) the "consumer" protocol type (though bear in mind that I have zero experience with golang). Assuming that's correct, I'm not sure what about these protocol definitions Kafka sees as mismatching. I'm guessing Kafka compares the `ProtocolType` strings of each consumer attempting to join a group, so maybe the golang consumer's `ProtocolType` is not a bytestring, but some unicode representation? That's a bit of a stretch perhaps.

For reference, [this](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/5345019ef3a66c1c93d0c05f4d3504045324e5c6/pykafka/protocol.py#L1357) is pykafka's `MemberAssignment` implementation, and [this](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/5345019ef3a66c1c93d0c05f4d3504045324e5c6/pykafka/protocol.py#L1198) is its `ConsumerGroupProtocolMetadata` implementation, sent along with `JoinGroupRequest`s.

The central question here is: what aspect of sarama and pykafka's protocol definitions does Kafka see as incompatible?
I'm glad you were able to discover that procedure via the available documentation. Any thoughts or PRs on how to improve the process of discovering that information are quite welcome.

Pykafka determines protocol version entirely on the basis of the user-supplied [broker version](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/2a98573bcdd1ab1382928d587aa0268a619a667e/pykafka/client.py#L91). For example, here's how the `Producer` [does it](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/2a98573bcdd1ab1382928d587aa0268a619a667e/pykafka/producer.py#L161). This check is pretty [simplistic](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/2a98573bcdd1ab1382928d587aa0268a619a667e/pykafka/utils/__init__.py#L31) at the moment, so it's possible/likely that the way we determine protocol version will need to be adjusted to allow it to be set independently from broker version. This has been fixed in the development version. The good news that this was an issue with the testing infrastructure, and was totally unrelated to photutils' functionality.

Your local workarounds could be either to downgrade pytest version to <3.2, or to install the development version of photutils.

When doing comparisons between IRAF and python it's important to note that IRAF uses 1-based indices, while Python uses 0-based indices.  That's means you will need to subtract 1 from your IRAF-based `x` and `y` positions when using `photutils`.  Have you done that?

This code was written for small Nside (=1 or 2!), so there were never a large number of pixels in our application.

Without changing the algorithm, you could try:

change ll_contains to ll_sorted_contains
change ll_append to ll_insert_ascending

this "ll" data structure is a block-list: linked-list of arrays, so the sorted inserts and checks can jump by the array size (256 here) rather than iterating through the whole list.  Technically doesn't improve the complexity, but it should improve the speed!

The Astrometry.net code does also include the "bt.c" code, which implement a similar API but uses an AVL tree under the hood, so should yield fast inserts and searches.  However, it is derived from GNU libavl, so probably won't work for your licensing situation.


More broadly: the algorithm here is expanding a frontier, finding healpix neighbours and checking them individually.  Obviously, as Nside increases, this is not a good idea.  As Nside increases, it becomes more like a regular grid, and you could use a different approach, say finding the start and end pixels within each row inside each base healpixel.

@JDWarner I am quite agnostic about this, actually, since you do alter the meaning. Say I imread a binary image called 'true-where-the-black-blobs-are.tif', I probably don't want the result to be False where the black blobs are.

We've mentioned metadata before and how we don't want to encumber skimage with it, but I think metadata is in fact the solution here.

Some options:

- imread_metadata: return metadata associated with an image file
- imread_with_metadata: return image and metadata as a tuple. (`metadata` is a dictionary.)
- imread(return_metadata=False): return an (image, metadata_dict) tuple when return_metadata is True.

Then we can do:
io.imshow(..., metadata=None)

and use the metadata to determine when to invert the colormap used.

Regarding your dataset size, since you are resizing your images to a single size, it's straightforward to compute the amount of memory needed: n_images * height * width * dtype_bytes. (The last number is 1 for uint8 images, 8 for float64, and 4 for float32.) That's the *bare minimum*, and things like a machine learning model etc. will also take up space in memory and need to be accounted for. For large dataset sizes, I strongly recommend you look at incremental machine learning in batches. See this document from scikit-learn:

http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/scaling_strategies.html#incremental-learning

which links to a relevant example classifying text documents:

http://scikit-learn.org/stable/auto_examples/applications/plot_out_of_core_classification.html

Obviously you are in a different field, but the relevant concepts should be similar. TensorFlow, Keras, PyTorch, etc, all have facilities for incremental machine learning.

To make your life easier I would first do a single pass saving the images in the correct format to disk, then I would do a second learning pass. Note that as pointed out in the above document, for many models, you will need to make sure that your first batch of images contains all target labels.

I'm going to close this issue as I don't think we have a memory leak, but do feel free to continue the discussion here.

```shell
    $ sudo pip install -U virtualenvwrapper setuptools pip
    if ! grep -q virtualenvwrapper ~/.bashrc; then
        mkdir -p "$HOME/.virtualenvs"
        echo "export WORKON_HOME=$HOME/.virtualenvs" >> "$HOME/.bashrc"
        echo "source $(which virtualenvwrapper.sh)" >> "$HOME/.bashrc"
    fi
    export WORKON_HOME=$HOME/.virtualenvs
    source "$(which virtualenvwrapper.sh)"
```

As I said, it is not convenient to keep track of a global vector where I push back every output.
My current workflow is the following:
1. build the computational graph: there is O(n^4)~O(n^6) outputs, generated by different modules
2. I accumulate all this outputs and sum them so I can do a bulk forward on the GPU
3. A dynamic program computes the structured hinge loss
4. I call incremental forward on this hinge loss
5. I call backward

I just wanted to know if it possible to get rid of step 2, so I don't have to keep around a global vector but still achieve the efficiency of the "bulk forward". I am not saying that I cannot do it, just that it is not convenient for when I have complicated dynamic networks.

Because you are using `serialized=True`, which means that the objects will be pickled before storing them in the HDF file.

I'm saying something like that, and there are a lot of variants on what `dill` will do, depending on the situation.  But, yes, the worst case is it pickles the entire array and everything it refers to.  If you want to see what's pickled in each case, turn on `trace`.

I believe it's: `dill.detect.trace(True)`

the hang seems to be related to the flake8 implementations usage of a multiprocessing queue - I'd bet if you hit a similar `PicklingError` in a worker in the venv context you'd probably see a likewise hang.

at least one topical workaround seems to be:

`$ pex 'flake8==3.0.0b2' -c flake8 -- -j1 setup.py`

which instructs flake8 to use a single core and runs successfully - this seems to confirm a multiprocess related bug.

as to the cause of the PicklingError itself, I'm guessing there's some fundamental difference in the way the class is loaded in the main runtime vs in multiprocessing worker.

if you run `pex 'flake8==3.0.0b2' -c flake8 -- -j2 setup.py` on your laptop then hit control-c, what does the raw traceback look like?

Then I found out about `cchardet`, which is a re-implementation of chardet with c-bindings. I returns this in a fraction of a second:
```
In [2]: cchardet.detect(open('/Users/adam/Downloads/PEF2016_AC01.csv').read())

So the reason this happens is that when a default virtualhost isn't specifically identified (for a given port; in this case 443), the _first_ virtualhost automatically becomes the default.

fwiw, this _exact_ behavior was reported early on for router v2 and was solved using the exact solution suggested here. We explicitly designated a default virtualhost with a self-signed cert. This became a "junk drawer" for responding with a 404 to requests that didn't match some other non-default vhost / application.

I'll leave it up to @bacongobbler and @mboersma whether they think it is worth backporting that improvement to Deis v1.x.

As discussed in [this comment from an old PR](https://github.com/chardet/chardet/pull/73#issuecomment-145955258) this is entirely the correct *and required* behavior. UTF-16LE and UTF-16BE are what one is required to report when there is no BOM, but they are not allowed if there is a BOM.

Looks like an easy fix, we just need to check that the returned headers for the DAO check have length 2 before unpacking them.

I don't think `validate_header_response` is the right solution here since an empty response is *valid* for a generic `GetBlockHeaders` request, it's just that in this specific case, we require the response to contain all of the results.  I think that we need two validation levels, one for the *objective* question of is it a valid response according to protocol, and then the subjective one of "does this response work for the reason we requested".  In this case, a simple `len(headers) == 2` check should suffice.

    is foreign system : False

I ran in to this issue myself and I'm really happy I found this report after spending way too long trying to find my own work around. Using `call` seemed to work as I expected. I was able to do stuff like:

    call activate myenv || echo It Failed

This was on a Windows 7 laptop.

Added a second commit to handle a second bug that could---again, under rare circumstances---cause `logic.minimize` to enter an infinite loop. It is caused when the starting solution provided to `minimize` is stale; e.g., because additional constraints have been added since the solution was generated. The code now detects this situation and recomputes a solution. We've also made it possible not to supply an initial point at all.

Did some intensive digging into this issue:
It boils down to prioritization of features in the solver logic. The specific catch for this issue: None of the packages actually use features 😵! Well, except for some old Python debug builds in `https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64`:
```
python: 2.7.9-d1    [debug]
python: 2.7.9-d2    [debug]
python: 2.7.10-d0   [debug]
python: 2.7.11-d0   [debug]
```
Of course none of those will ever be installed, especially for Python 3 environments.
`conda 4.4.0rc1` actually only succeeded coincidentally as those Python builds only specify their required feature but have no dependency on the package which provides that feature (also named `debug`). For some reason `conda 4.4.0rc1` will only behave like `conda 4.3.30` if they would correctly specify their dependency -- for now I couldn't pin down the cause of that behavioral difference since the feature support code changed a bit from `4.3.x` to `4.4.x`...
Nonetheless, I think those builds can be classified as broken nonetheless:
```
(ins)(root) [miniconda3]$  conda create --dry-run -n x python=2.7.9=d1

Use "conda info <package>" to see the dependencies for each package.

```

No.  That's how it's actually handled in Curator's command-line code.  That setting is parsed from the `option` block of the `action`, and then used to catch errors before proceeding accordingly.  The code for this behavior is [here](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.5.1/curator/cli.py#L160-L180).  `ignore_empty_list` is not handled in the action code itself, but between actions, based on how the previous action behaved.  This is why it is caught where it is.

We've seen this issue before, but only once or twice in our test environment. What's really puzzling is that we're checking if the thread is alive before calling `.join()`:

https://github.com/elastic/apm-agent-python/blob/e8b134d6af323c96b7ecc44b929a57bf696df524/elasticapm/base.py#L320-L323

Only thing I can imagine is that calling `.cancel()` sometimes makes the thread die immediately, so that the `.join()` right after fails. As `cancel()` only sets an `Event`, we can probably move it out of the `if` clause, that would fix it if this is indeed the root cause. Basically:

    def _stop_send_timer(self):
        if self._send_timer and not self._send_timer == threading.current_thread():
            self._send_timer.cancel()
            if self._send_timer.is_alive():
                self._send_timer.join()

I pushed this [change](https://github.com/elastic/apm-agent-python/commit/cd2bd2001) to a branch. Would you mind giving this a test run in your test environment under high load to see if it fixes it? You can install it like this:

For posterity, here's an example that worked for me:
```
import asyncio
import aiobotocore
import string
import random

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
session = aiobotocore.get_session(loop=loop)
ec2_client = session.create_client('ec2', region_name='us-east-1')
s3_client = session.create_client('s3')


async def create_ec2_instance():
    print('starting ec2 run_instance')
    resp = await ec2_client.run_instances(
        ImageId='ami-22ce4934',
        InstanceType='t2.nano',
        MinCount=1,
        MaxCount=1,
    )
    waiter = ec2_client.get_waiter('instance_running')
    print('waiting for all ec2 instances to be running')
    await waiter.wait(InstanceIds=[resp['Instances'][0]['InstanceId']])
    print('ec2 instances are running!')


async def create_s3_bucket():
    bucket_name = 'my-bucket-' + ''.join(random.choices(string.digits, k=6))
    print('starting s3 create_bucket {}'.format(bucket_name))
    await s3_client.create_bucket(Bucket=bucket_name)
    print('waiting for bucket to exist')
    waiter = s3_client.get_waiter('bucket_exists')
    await waiter.wait(Bucket=bucket_name)
    print('s3 bucket exists!')


loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.gather(
    create_ec2_instance(),
    create_s3_bucket(),
))

ec2_client.close()
s3_client.close()
loop.close()
```

The issue is not with `test_requires` per se; indeed there are plans to make it part of the package metadata specification so that `pip` could also access that information. Then, libraries could go back to declaring their test dependencies inside `setup.py` if they like. This is what the PyPA roadmap means.

The issue is with **setuptools**, as it continues to use the ancient `easy_install` to deal with dependencies. In other words, it's `setup.py install` and `setup.py test` that are obsolete. The irrelevance of `tests_require` is just an artifact of this, since no one besides setuptools can access that data currently.

> It was actually using 1.1 by default, however I forced it to 2.0.0 as per instructions from README seen above.

The README also instructs to use `pip`. It has been the recommended way to install packages for years now. Please read http://stackoverflow.com/questions/3220404/why-use-pip-over-easy-install.

> Django would be the other example, seen here.

Seen where? There is no `tests_require` or `test_requires` to be found anywhere in that file.

Believe is the twenty-third studio album by American singer-actress Cher, released on November 10, 1998 by Warner Bros. Records. The RIAA certified it Quadruple Platinum on December 23, 1999, recognizing four million shipments in the United States; Worldwide, the album has sold more than 20 million copies, making it the biggest-selling album of her career. In 1999 the album received three Grammy Awards nominations including "Record of the Year", "Best Pop Album" and winning "Best Dance Recording" for the single "Believe". It was released by Warner Bros. Records at the end of 1998. The album was executive produced by Rob Dickens. Upon its debut, critical reception was generally positive. Believe became Cher's most commercially-successful release, reached number one and Top 10 all over the world. In the United States, the album was released on November 10, 1998, and reached number four on the Billboard 200 chart, where it was certified four times platinum. The album featured a change in Cher's music; in addition, Believe presented a vocally stronger Cher and a massive use of vocoder and Auto-Tune. In 1999, the album received 3 Grammy Awards nominations for "Record of the Year", "Best Pop Album" and winning "Best Dance Recording". Throughout 1999 and into 2000 Cher was nominated and winning many awards for the album including a Billboard Music Award for "Female Vocalist of the Year", Lifelong Contribution Awards and a Star on the Walk of Fame shared with former Sonny Bono. The boost in Cher's popularity led to a very successful Do You Believe? Tour. The album was dedicated to Sonny Bono, Cher's former husband who died earlier that year from a skiing accident. Cher also recorded a cover version of "Love Is in the Air" during early sessions for this album. Although never officially released, the song has leaked on file sharing networks. ```py
def get_filter(self, user_id, filter_id):
    return self._send("GET",
                      "/user/{userId}/filter/{filterId}".format(userId=user_id, filterId=filter_id),
                      api_path=MATRIX_V2_API_PATH)
```

@tgoodlet I have something working mostly on my side that I will push in a branch of scancode-toolkit soon.

But in doing I found out that I may use only a few capabilities of pluggy. In particular I do not use pluggy to run the plugins, only as a registry. And my plugins are using Click for the CLI "UI".

The way dependencies are expressed is on other plugins is that each plugin holds a list of `project_name: plugin_name` strings where `plugin_name` is the setuptools entrypoint name. This is only an attribute of my plugins, not pluggy. Based onthis and the actual execution context of user-selected options:
1. I can check if a plugin is enabled (plugin-specific, typically based on its user-selected options or else)
2. If yes, I can check if all its dependencies are also enabled, recursively, by walking the dependencies graph, detecting and erroring on cycles, and checking if each plugin is enabled.
3. If yes, the plugin can run, otherwise an error is raised as dependencies are not met.

So all in all, it may be of little value for pluggy and pytest. And frankly I have no idea on how dependencies are handled by pluggy, though I would be glad to use this.

Anyway, I will ping you here when I push this.

It now happens that in `boulder/ra/registration-authority.go` the `func (ra *RegistrationAuthorityImpl) validateContacts(...` calls `validateEmail` which splits the domain-part from the local-part of the e-mail address and then calls `resolver.LookupHost(domain)`. **This method tries to resolve an IP-Address for the domain-name** and that fails.

I have found nowhere in the documentation that an A-Record for the domainname is required for letsencrypt to work. And I can not though of any valid reason for this, but I'm sure I'm missing something here.

With "domain name has an IP" I mean sth like this in bind-syntax:

```
@       IN      SOA     ns.example.de. postmaster.example.de. (
                2015111801      ; serial number
                3H              ; refresh, seconds
                2H              ; retry, seconds
                1W              ; expire, seconds
                1H ) 
                IN      NS     ns.xxxxxxxxxxx.de
                IN      NS     ns2.xxxxxxxxxxx.de
                IN      A       789.654.321.123
```

1. No CDN support for apex domains

Okay, so the "clone-and-auto" doesn't use most of the source it clones. Fine to close this issue once a new release of `letsencrypt-apache` is made.
the `/live/` directory contains symlinks that gets updated eacht time you run letsencrypt-auto. So just link these and you're good to go.

It looks like you're running le-auto in a vagrant box, and vagrant (IIRC) shares the vagrant home dir with the host—in your case, Windows. le-auto tries to remove the old version of itself to move the new one overtop, but the underlying file is a Windows file, and Windows hates that sort of thing. To avoid this, park the le-auto file outside `~vagrant`.

Consider the following alternative (which I don't say is superior or anything -- just worth considering):

optional argument 'encrypt' instead of optional argument 'prompt'


![prompt vs encrypt](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13263928/37176057-3e632822-22e9-11e8-9cd2-533fc7847706.png)

There are a couple of cases where various errors can be returned by poll() if the connection(s) to the coordinator or partition leader broker goes down (internal error codes), or if the cluster is rebalancing itself (external error codes).

In the latter case, the external error codes coming from the brokers, we can't really know the exact error code that we'll see. So it might be better to not exit the consumer if an error is returned by poll(), there's a big chance that it will recover automatically once brokers are back up.

I'm not really opposed to these changes either way, but I can't replicate the issue.

This only solves the problem for the trivial case of fixed offsets, and in fact will give the *wrong* answer in the more common case of non-fixed offsets, since it makes no effort to round-trip the actual `tzinfo` object used (or equivalent).

Consider this:

```python
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil import rrule, tz

ET = tz.gettz('America/New_York')
rr = rrule.rrule(rrule.MONTHLY, dtstart=datetime(2017, 1, 15, 12, tzinfo=ET), count=12)
rr_rt = rrule.rrulestr(str(rr))

for dt1, dt2 in zip(rr, rr_rt):
    assert dt1 == dt2, (str(dt1), str(dt2))
```

With your patch, the result is:

> AssertionError: ('2017-03-15 12:00:00-04:00', '2017-03-15 12:00:00-05:00')

As a general rule, I think that using datetime-aware timezones in `rrule` is not a great idea.

@mr-c

> I think adding an optional top-level version field to CommandLineTool should be enough. Everything else can be extracted from the baseCommand.

I don't think that is true - many tools or at least some tools - if they are going to play well in Galaxy-land at least - are going to depend on multiple underlying packages. For instance, nearly every Galaxy mapper tool uses samtools to sort output BAM files are the initial command. Your own (formerish) khmer tools for instance have multiple dependencies - https://github.com/galaxyproject/tools-iuc/blob/master/tools/khmer/macros.xml. If we say that CWL will never be the implementation for stuff like this - then I cannot really make the argument that CWL will someday replace Galaxy tools. 
I have argued from the beginning that our focus needs to be on creating useful "boxes" not modelling the exact CLI commands - I stand by that. I have an agenda that is different than your agenda - but hopefully we can make both work with the same standard.

> environment modules aren't typically shared between sites; package names aren't standardized at all; and they rarely work at other sites let alone cross platform :-)

As a... lets call it dependency injection technique it works on CentOS derived things, Debian derived things, FreeBSD derived things. It is far more cross platform than ``.deb`` stuffs. Surely, it will take some time and effort to adapt but if you are an institution with environment modules - you probably have the software professionals capable of doing this.

There is work that could be done - and will be done - to make this more easy within galaxy-lib - namely allowing site-specific remapping of requirement names and versions. We have more of a blue-sky scenario to get it right with CWL but even in the Galaxy landscape this technique has proven feasible (ping @pvanheus has deployed tools this way I believe for instance).

> Can galaxy.tools.deps.requirements.ToolRequirement be spun out of galaxylib into its own lib?

Discussed on gitter - it can be done if needed - I can't think of a technical reason to do it however.

Ok, I see what is going on now, but I do think it is a real problem.

From the compiler's perspective, `DistriOptimizer.optimize` requires a `dataset: DistributedDataset[MiniBatch[T]]`. However, what you're pointing out is that what has to be passed in is not just any `DistributedDataset[MiniBatch[T]]`, but a dataset that has been "transformed" from a `DistributedDataset[Sample[T]]` or something at a per sample basis. This is because when transformations are applied via `Dataset.transform`, the size method simply references `preDataset.size()`. So the "size" of a distributed dataset is the size of whatever dataset originally created it. 

I would argue that this is highly deceiving. But regardless, there is a problem since users can pass in a completely valid dataset to the optimize function and get incorrect behavior. If you are not supposed to be allowed to create your own minibatch dataset, then APIs to do so must be made private or we would need more robust type checking on the optimization functions.

I guess this begs the question of what is a "DataSet" in BigDL exactly? Right now, the "size" of a dataset is simply whatever the number of elements in the original Dataset were, even if I perform arbitrary transformations on it. How should anyone know that the size will trace back to the original dataset in the lineage? The documentation for it is:

````scala
/**
   * Total size of the data set
   * @return
   */
  def size(): Long
````

This is basically incorrect. The doc should say something like "The number of elements in the dataset, or in the very first dataset in the lineage if this dataset is the result of transformations." 

I would propose to solve this particular issue by changing `DistriOptimizer` to not use `dataset.size()`, since it's unpredictable in its output. Instead, you could just count the number of elements in each minibatch by using:

````scala
val totalSamples = dataset.data(train = false).map(_.getTarget().toTensor.size().head).reduce(_ + _)
````

Then, we can correct the tests to produce the correct behavior and also add unit tests to verify this explicitly. What do you think?

Yes that fixed the problem. Now I'm running tests to see if it broke anything

Currently data streams will be written sequentially, e.g.,., all data from one DataChunkIterator will be written before the next dataset gets written. This means, while multiple data streams are supported, the write happens in sequence, one stream after the other. This is fine in situation where the full stream is available (e.g., when converting data) but in particular for situations where data is generated simultaneously online (e.g., when recording data during an experiment) this can be limiting. This ticket describes the need, to be able to write from multiple data streams simultaneously. Technically this is challenging for several reasons:

* When writing multiple chunked datasets simultaneously one (or serveral) chunks-at-a-time would likely lead to fragmentation of the datasets in HDF5 files (which can be problematic for performance)
* Requires support to delay the write of select datasets 
* Requires advanced error handling (e.g., how should the write behave when one of the streams has an error?)
* etc.

Indeed, the `alias` is used in many places in the WebUI to display a "friendly" name for the objects. If the alias is not defined, it is replaced with the object name. This is why your realm, which has no alias, looks like having one ...

I think this behavior is a quite good solution and I will make not change for this.

Because now index.js is doing memory management... it will hold some memory buffers, renew the content of the buffers for new frames, and release the buffers when it's time.

It will also hold frameset/frames to avoid multiple (e.g. thousands of) JavaScript objects to be involved in regular new/GC cycle.

Hello @doisyg ,
It is not clear what do you mean by `crash of uvc driver` - can you provide kernel/application logs taken during this event ?

According to the timestamps the reports are fired 32 seconds after connecting R200 to Host or Boot-up. I.e. it occurred immediately upon application start. Connecting it to the crash that occurs once in 24 hours seem unreasonable.

As to the error itself - it is one of standard UVC controls (e.g Gain,Exposure) that could not be applied in a timely manner.
Linux UVC driver does not support async controls so Librealsense would retry the same command in loop till a proper response is received/timeout. (you may notice 50ms periods between retries). This situation is not standard yet quite common, and does not indicate functionality issues.

There are two ways to do this:
  - Make the categorizer not output empty categories.
  - Make the dashboards not render empty categories.

The first approach would lead to our standard Polymer-induced data
binding issues (as described in, e.g., #170), so we should try not to do
this.

The second approach would be possible at the cost of additional plugin
code:
```diff
diff --git a/tensorboard/plugins/scalar/tf_scalar_dashboard/tf-scalar-dashboard.html b/tensorboard/plugins/scalar/tf_scalar_dashboard/tf-scalar-dashboard.html
index c99eae1..c558ec2 100644
--- a/tensorboard/plugins/scalar/tf_scalar_dashboard/tf-scalar-dashboard.html
+++ b/tensorboard/plugins/scalar/tf_scalar_dashboard/tf-scalar-dashboard.html
@@ -127,6 +127,7 @@ limitations under the License.
           </paper-input>
           <template is="dom-repeat" items="[[_categories]]" as="category">
             <tf-category-pane category="[[category]]">
+              <template is="dom-if" if="[[!category.empty]]">
                 <tf-paginated-view
                   items="[[category.items]]"
                   pages="{{category._pages}}"
@@ -151,6 +152,7 @@ limitations under the License.
                     </template>
                   </template>
                 </tf-paginated-view>
+              </template>
             </tf-category-pane>
           </template>
         </template>

```
(`category.empty` would need to be added to the categorizer output, but
that's easy.)

(That immediate area already goes four templates deep, but that doesn't
necessarily mean that this is the wrong thing to do.)

@arborin - This looks like a transport issue. Have you configured the serial port speed, parity, etc to match something that the printer understands? Correct serial port settings should be listed in the vendor manual.

Once you use `stty` to apply the correct settings, you should be able to send text:

```bash
stty ...
echo "Hello World" >> /dev/ttyUSB0
```

See "[What is the easiest way to configure serial port on Linux?](https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/242778/what-is-the-easiest-way-to-configure-serial-port-on-linux)" on Unix StackExchange for usage. Once it works on the command-line, you should be able to do the same thing using [the available Printer.serial()](https://github.com/python-escpos/python-escpos/blob/development/src/escpos/printer.py#L110) from python.

Summarizing my understanding based on previous discussions:

- We're getting a bad grade because Nginx is willing to do 1024-bit DHE with 2048-bit RSA keys
- There's no problem using shared params with 2048-bit keys
- We should pre-generate and ship shared params, then set `ssl_dhparam` to a file containing the results of a precomputed param generation, which is done by executing `openssl dhparam -out dhparams.pem 2048`

Some implementation notes:

`argparse`'s `add_argument` defaults to `None` when `action` is not specified and defaults to the inverse of store_true or store_false when either of those is the action, thus many defaults are initialized in `CLI_DEFAULTS` but not explicitly assigned in `cli.py`. I think it's redundant, but let me know if it'd be stylistically preferred to assign them in the `add_argument` call too.

Before I remove [WIP], I want to be sure about reliable defaults for:
- `cert_path`: If the CLI verb is "certonly", the `auth_cert_path` default is used. If the verb is "revoke", an argument is required. If it's neither, it's sometimes required, and if not, it'll default to `None`. I need to figure out which default to use for `cert_path` when not taking CLI args.
- `fullchain_path` and `chain_path`: similar duality as `cert_path` above.
- `domains`: During tests, the "domains" argument would carry arguments from previous argument parsings only if using `flag_defaults('domains')`. Several tests fail, but `ParseTest.test_parse_domains` demonstrates it best. Both example.com and trailing.period.com appear in the second parsing.

If the dream is to boil down boilerplate to a line like `self.config = mock.MagicMock(**constants.CLI_DEFAULTS)`, the above have to be addressed. I think it'd be easiest to resolve those path arguments during the `__init__` of `NamespaceConfig`, instead of trying to shoehorn them into static defaults. My very minimal start to this issue is in `bytes-fullchain-bites` which should fix the problem on the `acme` side for ACMEv2. We still need to:

1. Have the backwards compatible client set `fullchain_pem` to `unicode`/`str` for ACMEv1.

Only other note is a function like `acme.crypto_util.dump_pyopenssl_chain` should almost certainly still return `bytes`. Why? If the function supports DER encoding (which that function does), it can't return `unicode`/`str` because that's a binary format that doesn't necessarily contain all Unicode characters. We could change the return type based on the encoding requested, but that seems like a bad idea to me.

> @mbolivar Hmm, I have given it a couple of tries, but I can not make sense of the current pyOCD runner test case. I am not used to Python and have no knowledge of the test framework used.

No worries, let's see if we can figure it out together.

**This patch:**

You're adding a `--frequency <freq>` argument to the runner and a `frequency=<freq>` constructor keyword argument. If neither is present, no changes should be made to the pyocd command lines. If `--frequency` is given on the west command line and/or the `frequency` argument is given to the constructor, we need a `-f <freq>` option passed to pyocd.

**How the tests work:**

The test matrix works like this:

1. a pyocd runner is instantiated from just the constructor (`__init__()`)
  1.1. with values specifed for every kwarg (`TEST_ALL_KWARGS`)
  1.2. with all kwargs left at default values (`TEST_DEF_KWARGS`)
2. runner is instantiated from the command line (`create()`)
  2.1. with values specified for every optional argument (`TEST_ALL_PARAMS`)
  2.2. with all optional command line arguments left unset (`TEST_DEF_PARAMS`)

For each of the 4 runner types above, the `flash`, `debug`, and `debugserver` commands are run.

The test code is instrumented to capture the subprocess invocations (`FLASH_ALL_EXPECTED_CALL` is the expected command for both `TEST_ALL_KWARGS` and `TEST_ALL_PARAMS`, and similarly for the `DEBUG_xxx_EXPECTED_yyy`, `DEBUGSERVER_xxx_EXPECTED_yyy` lists).

**What needs to be done:**

Since frequency support is being added as a kwarg and as an optional command line argument, this means `TEST_ALL_KWARGS` and `TEST_ALL_PARAMS` need to be extended to use the `frequency` kwarg and `--frequency` command line option, respectively.

If you do that and then run the tests (shippable will run them if you re-push, or you can run `python3 setup.py test` as shown in the README), I expect the tests to fail, because the pyocd command lines will now include some unexpected `-f` options in them.

So you can then check the test output to see what was expected, and what happened instead. That should give you a hint of how to update the `..._EXPECTED_...` variables (and which ones to change) to account for the new option.

Hope this helps! Let me know if about any other questions.

It looks like the problem is that the directory `/tmp/ray` is created in `ray.init`, but I think we are attempting to create a file in that directory in the `DQN` constructor, which happens before the call to `ray.init`. In fact, currently there is no call to `ray.init`.

We could add a call to `ray.init` before creating the DQN object for now, but we'll still run into this problem when they happen out of order. Maybe we should just additionally attempt to create the `/tmp/ray` directory in the `Algorithm` constructor when we use it.

The problem there is that one of the two pubkeys in the multisig script starts with `0x00`, which is an invalid prefix for Bitcoin pubkeys. We need to decide whether a multisig script (by the way, also P2PK scripts can suffer from this) should still be considered a multisig even if the keys it includes are invalid.
